results.title: How to Write a Libretto: Suggestions and Guidelines
results.content:
Numbers need to be written out: since words need to be set to music and broken up into syllables, they need to be written out as words. For example, the sentence, “They sailed the 7 seas in 1864 on August 4th at 4:00pm.” could be written, “They sailed the seven seas in eighteen sixty-four on August fourth at four in the afternoon.” If you choose to use actual Arabic numbers or Roman numerals in your libretto, just be aware that the composer (or you) will need to write all of that out for setting to music. [...] Multisyllabic words need to be hyphenated. When a composer sets words to music, if the word has multiple syllables, it will have to be broken up with hyphens. For example, the following sentence, “I absolutely love writing opera librettos,” will be hyphenated like this: “I ab-so-lute-ly love writ-ing o-pe-ra li-bret-tos.” If you are writing a libretto for a composer and want to be a composer’s best friend, give them a hyphenated version of your text! One of my favorite online tools for hyphenating text is Juicio Brennan’s Hyphenator. It’s not absolutely perfect, so you’ll need to review your text after your hyphenate it, especially if you use a few uncommon words, but it definitely saves a lot of time. [...] 4. Trigger warnings: these aren’t necessary unless you choose to include them or feel you need them, but they usually go before the libretto begins.
3. Libretto: this is the core of the text and contains the entire libretto.
4. Copyright: I know this may seem obvious, but it’s always a good idea to put a copyright notice at the end of your libretto, for example, © Copyright [year] [Full Name].

results.title: Libretto - Wikipedia
results.content:
Wikipedia
The Free Encyclopedia

## Contents

# Libretto

A libretto (from the Italian word libretto, lit. 'booklet') is the text used in, or intended for, an extended musical work such as an opera, operetta, masque, oratorio, cantata or musical. The term libretto is also sometimes used to refer to the story line of a ballet or the texts of major liturgical works, such as the Mass "Mass (liturgy)"), requiem, or sacred cantata. [...] |  v  t  e  Musical composition and song | |
 --- |
| Roles |  Composer   + List  Lyricist  Songwriter  Singer-songwriter  Hymnwriter |
| Components |  Instrumentation "Instrumentation (music)")  Libretto   + Electronic libretto  Lyrics  Musical form  Musical notation  Orchestration  Song cycle | [...] Printed scores for operas naturally contain the entire libretto, although there can exist significant differences between the score and the separately printed text. More often than not, this involves the extra repetition of words or phrases from the libretto in the actual score. For example, in the aria "Nessun dorma" from Puccini's Turandot, the final lines in the libretto are "Tramontate, stelle! All'alba, vincerò!" (Fade, you stars! At dawn, I will win!). However, in the score they are sung as "Tramontate, stelle! Tramontate, stelle! All'alba, vincerò! Vincerò! Vincerò!".
